title: Air-sealing electrical sockets and back-boxes in external walls
subtitle: Membrane seals and flexible mastic to stop cold air paths through socket positions
excerpt: Every socket back-box in an external wall is a hole through the plasterboard and into the wall cavity. In a cavity wall home, each one leaks warm air outward and draws cold air in. Sealing them with flexible back-box membrane covers and mastic at the perimeter takes around 20 minutes per socket and costs pennies per fitting.
sourceNotes: AECB (Association for Environment Conscious Building) airtightness detailing guides. Passivhaus Institute detailing guidance on socket sealing. Energy Saving Trust 'Improving airtightness' technical brief.

glossaryTerms.definition: The plastic or metal box sunk into a wall to contain a socket or switch mechanism. Where sited in an external wall, it typically creates a continuous air path from the room to the cavity or behind the plaster.

Uncontrolled movement of air through gaps and cracks in the building envelope. Air leakage carries warmth out in winter and draws cold air in, and is responsible for a significant portion of heat loss in older UK homes.

content.text: On a cold windy day, hold your hand near a socket on an external wall. In most pre-1990s homes, you will feel a draught at the faceplate or around its edge. The
back-box
punches through the plasterboard and sits in or against the cavity.
Air leakage

through sockets is small per fitting, but a typical three-bedroom semi has 30 to 50 sockets and switches on external walls. Taken together, they account for a measurable fraction of the total infiltration rate.
Safety first

Turn off the circuit at the consumer unit before removing any faceplate. Confirm the socket is dead with a plug-in tester or voltage tester before touching terminals. Do not proceed if you are uncertain whether the correct breaker is off.
What you will need

Electrical socket membrane seals, sometimes sold as 'back-box insulation covers' or 'socket draught-sealing membranes'. These are pre-cut foam or polyethylene sheet pads with a central hole for the wiring.
Flexible mastic for sealing the gap between back-box edge and plasterboard
A small brush or spatula to work mastic into corners
Step by step
With the circuit off and confirmed dead, unscrew the faceplate using a flathead screwdriver. Do not disconnect any wires. Note: most faceplates have two screws; some have a push-fit mechanism.

Examine the gap between the back-box and the plasterboard around the perimeter. In an older house, this is often open. Apply flexible mastic into this gap, working it in with a small brush. The mastic must remain flexible to accommodate movement; standard silicone is too rigid.

Slide the membrane seal over the wires so it sits flat against the wall surface, behind the faceplate. The foam seal compresses between faceplate and plaster when you screw the faceplate back on, creating a compression seal around the whole perimeter.
Refix the faceplate, tightening the screws enough to compress the foam without bowing the faceplate. Restore the circuit.

Check the result on a cold windy day: hold your hand near the faceplate edge. The draught should be gone. Repeat for every socket and switch on external walls, including light switches at external wall positions.
Prioritising which sockets to seal

Ground-floor sockets on the external walls of a suspended timber floor house are the highest priority; they sit above a ventilated void and the air path from underfloor to socket is completely open. Upper-floor sockets in cavity-wall construction are next. Internal-wall sockets on party walls have a smaller but real air path into the cavity and are worth sealing if you are doing a systematic pass.

Check the cable entry at the back of the box. A 20 mm conduit or cable hole in the box back that is not grommet-filled is a direct air path into the cavity. Seal with a grommet or a piece of foam pressed into the entry.

attrs.symptom: Faceplate does not sit flush with membrane in place

content.text: The membrane is too thick for a shallow back-box. Use the thinner foil-backed version rather than the foam pad version; it adds less bulk.
